That said, the game is challenging, both in gameplay and navigation. Use your tools as often as your wits and reflexes and you'll have a great time. I must confess however, I'm not the most skilled and stuck on the final few fights. Your milage may vary.

Elden Ring is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ beautiful.

Not only beautiful in its art direction, but in it's combat system, it's approach to independence, the music, and the way it makes you feel. Beating that hard ass boss that you've been stuck on for 2 days? Amazing. Getting a new weapon or sorcery that deletes the next boss? Hell yeah.

Exploration, in the sense of doing dungeons, trying new weapons, etc., is the entire point, and rewards you handsomely. You find some hidden mechanics or skill set that resonates with you and end up carrying it to NG+ because it's so damn fun to use. This game has the sexiest and most fun weapons in ANY souls/soulslike game.

Honestly, I took way too long to get into this game and regret not doing so sooner. It's been over a week since I 100% the achievements and I still want to go back to start a completely different build. This was probably the most addicting gaming experience I've had in the past decade, and I could see myself coming back to this game yearly, it's just that good.

Elden Ring as a game has many baffling design decisions, many of which somehow work. Typical of Souls games, the story is not straightforward and instead told primarily through the environment, flavour text in items and the enemy designs. In terms of visuals and storytelling, the game rewards exploration, attention to detail and perseverance (be prepared to do many awfully designed quests for your lore!)

The quest design is some of the worst we've seen in any Souls game. Anyone who thinks the quests are well designed in Elden Ring I am convinced they have not tried to do a quest without a wiki. Several NPCs will hint to where they will be next, but the location is so generic and nonspecific you could actively search for them and miss them because, SURPRISE they were tucked away in this little corner behind this rock you forgot to look behind. If the hardest part of your quests are figuring out where to find the next parts to continue it, your quests are badly designed.

It's strong points in gameplay are the high levels of build customisation, including large varieties of weapons, skills, talismans and armour. Unfortunately, there are many points where it feels like player choice was not prioritised to the detriment of the game. Several weapons are seemingly stuck with skills that aren't even unique to them. Many skills are badly designed, clearly meant more for show than actual practical use, and if they were made better they would make for interesting additions to builds.

Although I really like skills and spells in Elden Ring, they cause a majority of the balance problems, despite how fun they are. Many skills/spells can easily be slotted into any build for immediate power due to low stat requirements. Any build is just a few steps away from broken territory, even the simplest, most basic ones. If you're just using a big bonk stick, you'll quickly realise that jump attacks are insanely overpowered in PvE, easily staggering large enemies and bosses. There are a handful of "no-brainer" talismans that are so strong you might as well always have them equipped unless you really need to squeeze in something for your build.

This ultimately leads to my point that Elden Ring is a very unbalanced game with many overcentralising mechanics. It's no wonder the community is so uptight about how you play this game. Ultimately, I think balance of single player games still matters. I would prefer more balanced options that are competitive with each other, and also less "no-brainer" choices that are just the obvious picks.

In the end, Elden Ring is a Souls game, and in Souls games knowledge is power. Practically every Souls game has mechanics where if you know how to use them you can utterly break the game, but Elden Ring is so full of them it's very easy to just accidentally happen upon them.

I would just prepare for 20 hours of learning what weapon/build you truly want to go with before thinking you will actually progress significantly in the game's story. Explore and get your levels by doing small dungeons/caves along the way. Go off paths but watch for bloodstains and messages that will inform you with good or bad information. Just explore this world and you will be rewarded, memorize boss move sets, position and don't always rely on lock on to dodge attacks.

The first time I played this game I got to the first real batch of enemies and died so many times I uninstalled and refunded the game. About a year later I thought I'd give it another shot. Now I've put over 300 hours into the game, and I'd say it's one of my favorite games of all time. 

This was my first Souls game, and it took some getting used to. With a beginner's guide and the right mindset, you too can learn to embrace the pain and frustration and learn to love it.

Elden Ring, due to the open world, basically allows you to choose your difficulty. You can over-level for every single boss if you want. Or you can run to the bosses with no gear and try to kill them without getting hit. It's up to you.
 
This game, more than almost any other I've played, rewards you for everything you learn. Every time you die, you get a little better, and that is incredibly satisfying. 

The only thing I dislike and could say is a flaw is the platforming. There are times when the geometry will move you or you might not jump as far as you think. Just accept that you'll have stupid deaths from falling off ledges. 

I highly recommend this game to others who aren't sure about Souls games and would like to try them out.

That said, it’s the mechanics of DS2 without the thousands of bugs, and it’s so easy that it actually makes me sad that every boss has an attack pattern designed to be observed and for the player to learn how to play the game, yet it can be completely ignored with any optimized build for any class—Strength with its absurd staggers, Magic with its range advantage, and Bleed because, well, it’s Bleed.

Elden Ring completely pulled me in from the moment I first stepped into Limgrave. Seeing that massive world open up for the first time was honestly overwhelming in the best possible way. I remember just standing there, trying to decide where to go first, immediately making the questionable decision to wander off and explore [spoiler] before arriving at the first church [/spoiler]. That feeling of freedom is what makes this game so special. Every road, ruin, cave, or suspicious corner felt worth checking out because the game constantly rewarded curiosity. Even after hundreds of hours, I still kept finding hidden items, new paths, or entire areas I somehow missed before.

The bosses and world design were easily my favorite parts. Getting destroyed by the Tree Sentinel early on was humiliating but hilarious, and it perfectly taught me what kind of game I was facing.

Finally defeating Malenia after all the frustration, scarlet rot, and repeated failures felt incredibly satisfying. The game feels difficult, but fair, and every victory actually feels earned. I also loved diving into the lore, (even if most of it came from outside sources rather than the game directly - which is a personal choice in the end) 

Compared to Dark Souls III, this game takes everything I loved and pushes it much further. The open world, the freedom to explore, and even small quality-of-life improvements like traveling freely between Sites of Grace made the experience feel bigger and smoother without losing the soul of a FromSoftware game. Elden Ring may not be the hardest or fastest Soulslike ever, but it absolutely feels like one of the most memorable. It is one of those rare games where getting lost is not frustrating because it is the entire point.

This is FromSoftware's greatest game yet. The story is great, but the way it’s told follows the classic FromSoft method, or should I say Hidetaka Miyazaki’s method. The general story is presented clearly, but the deeper lore can feel tedious to uncover since you learn it through item descriptions and NPC dialogue. Still, it’s also fun piecing the lore together bit by bit, so I hope you have a keen eye.

The gameplay, however, is outstanding. It’s fluid and versatile, you can be a meta enjoyer, a casual player, a cheeser, or even a masochist; all of it is possible. I just wish they would release a second DLC, because the base game and SoTE still aren’t enough for me.

Elden Ring is truly one of the best games I have ever played.
The world is beautiful and full of mystery, with an incredible amount of variety in weapons, builds, bosses, and locations. Every area feels unique and rewarding to explore.

For fans of Souls games, this is an unforgettable experience filled with challenge, freedom, and adventure. I highly recommend everyone to give this masterpiece a chance and enjoy one of the most unique gaming experiences ever made.

In my humblest of opinions, Elden Ring is kinda... My favorite is dark souls II so make whatever assumptions you'd like, but after I first beat the game I was left feeling really meh. I couldn't bring myself to replay it the same way I want to replay DS2 or Sekiro, but I have regardless to make new characters or play with friends, just never beaten it again. Fromsoft really pushed themselves with this game, and it shows. They struggled with enemy variety in that I want to say 40% of enemies are reskinned version of other enemies, and infamously, each miniboss appears five or so times at least across the entire game. Exploration doesn't really reward the player with much, with most rewards being basic loot you can find on enemies or flowers or berry bushes and stuff. The world, however, feels a little empty. Even though dying worlds are their formula, it's really out of place here in my opinion. The game's lore revolves around the fact that the lands between are a lived in world full of colorful diverse characters, and yet I found most NPCs woefully out of place. The capitol was a huge let down for me, for example, and the roundtable hold is plain strange in my opinion, with merchants who you'll want to move between (one selling smithing stones and the other providing upgrades) being on polar opposite sides of the hub... Oh my lord and the dungeons - the dungeons as a whole are a perfect example for how tired I think the devs were while working on this game because the dungeons are the lamest and least inspired parts of this game. They are essentially the same structure with the same enemies for the most part, with ♥♥♥♥ loot at the end oh my lord. Each of them follows the same formula as well, really sad in my opinion. 

Fromsoft tried to accomplish A LOT of things with this title and I think they landed securely in an in-between place where there are great and egregious things at once, but mostly, the game is good.

I want to throw some serious shade at Shadow of the Erdtree DLC. What the hell man. It was just a slog, the areas were uninteresting, others felt uninspired and kind of pointless. The boss movesets seemed AI generated if I'm being honest, felt so unpredictable. Maybe I just wasn't good enough but holy hell the amount of shockwaves and double hit attacks (where you dodge the first part but get hit by the second while you're coming out of a roll) were commonplace. The hippo boss was insane, and I mean that in a good and bad way. It's because of all these facets I never actually reached Mesmer, just got so bogged down by those aforementioned aspects compounding onto me throughout my playtime. I've beaten Nioh 1 and 2 and those have some INSANE and irritating bosses but Shadow of the Erdtree gave me little to feel excited about in terms of pushing through. Oh my lord and the DAMAGE they deal is bonkers. Unironically, my favorite boss in all of Elden Ring was Godrick the Grafted - so much personality, BEST soundtrack in the game IMO, cool concept and phases, but whose moveset and behavior didn't feel chaotic, or plain silly like some of the bosses felt. Call me a normie, but that's my hill I guess.

Anyways after all that ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, it's still a fantastic game, just a flawed one, as they all are. I hope that Fromsoft either improves on their tried and true formula with ideas to keep it fresh (meaning dead worlds etc) or either tries really hard to remedy what made Elden Ring suffer in my eyes - feeling empty and feeling un-lived in. I also hope they take a look back at their masterpieces like Sekiro to reach a better synthesis of their different combat systems. Elden Ring at times could've really benefitted from being more like Sekiro, but instead just felt like DS3, even with guard counters.

I feel like it's such an important thing to discuss what makes these games feel "intimidating" at first. After my 2nd playthrough, I gotta say, this is one of THE best games I have ever played. And I am bummed at myself for holding off on playing for so long because how the community and others have made the game sound like it's impossible and too tough to understand.

What I've been encouraging my friends with is to just give this game at least 6 hours of chance, let yourself understand the basics of leveling up attributes and learn what "builds" are and weapon scaling. Everything beyond that is yours to explore in the way you want to. There's little homework to be done and more freedom.

The combat isn't too difficult if you let yourself explore the world everything it's got to offer so that you can upgrade your character and builds. The game and it's world design does a really good job at putting you in the exact locations where you need to be, and there is always a choice of coming back stronger to certain areas if it wasn't.

There's a fun challenge in all of the bosses, none of them are "impossible" to beat, you just have to be smart going in what's needed for the fight. Let yourself try hours at a single boss.

I can't really say much else other than there are enough ways to make the game easy for yourself. And I encourage new players (like me) to give these games a try.

Elden Ring isn’t just a game — it’s a cathedral of chaos, wonder, and triumph carved into the soul of modern gaming. From the first step into Limgrave to the final clash beneath shattered gold, every moment feels like walking through a living myth painted by gods and madmen. The world is breathtakingly vast, dripping with mystery, beauty, and terror around every corner. Every castle, every crypt, every forgotten battlefield tells a story without begging for attention — it simply exists, daring you to uncover its secrets.

FromSoftware didn’t create a map; they forged an odyssey. The combat is razor-sharp, brutal, and endlessly rewarding, demanding mastery while making every victory feel earned in blood and fire. Boss fights are spectacles of pure legend — monstrous, majestic, and unforgettable. Few games capture the feeling of true adventure, but Elden Ring resurrects it with overwhelming grandeur.

This is not merely open-world design; this is art, ambition, and excellence fused together in one merciless masterpiece. It respects your intelligence, tests your patience, and rewards your courage like no other. Elden Ring stands as a towering monument to what gaming can achieve when creativity is unchained.

A once-in-a-generation masterpiece. Beautiful. Savage. Eternal.
10/10 — a crown jewel forged in the flames of greatness.

This was my first ever FromSoftware game, and it was a great introduction to the souls games. Saying that this game is a 10/10 is an understatement. The open world is truly magical, straight out of a fantasy book (thanks George R. R. Martin). I can't go 10 steps on Torrent without finding a dungeon, beating the boss, and get either a mushroom, or a god-slaying weapon as a reward. The combat is stellar, and I could tell that the variety of builds is huge with the range of armaments, arts of war, talismans, and spells. Since this was my first souls game, the bosses were an extremely difficult, and the moment I tried to recuperate during combat, the bosses did not hold back to attack. Every boss and enemy spiked my focus, learning and avoiding their moves/abilities. All these factors would be for naught without the incredible music. Some of my favorite music comes from bosses such as Mohg or Godfrey, and the ambient music from Leyndell was not only soothing, but told a story of a great city in ruins after a war.
There were many times of frustration, but the game improved my perseverance and patience. Before fighting Malenia, I thought I would beat her within 10 tries. 10 tries turned to 20 tries, and 20 tries turned to 2 weeks. Even after many defeats, I stilled fought, and through patience I eventually gained Malenia's Great Rune. 
To sum it up, it's a pretty fun game.

I think I am on my 5th play-through of this game. Forgot to review at launch. Not sure if I have anything new to add.

Almost every aspect of this game is 11/10. Its legendary, probably one of the best of all time. The art direction and overall atmosphere is pretty much unmatched, maybe only by Bloodborne. The legacy dungeons have some of the best level designs in all the souls games.

My only major gripe is that the open world gets very dull after a while and I think they could have shrunk it by 25%-50% and achieved a more enjoyable experience. Not by cutting levels, but reducing the empty space between POIs. I would guess that at least 40% of my gameplay is combing the overworld for items with Torrent. I do not enjoy this but have an obsessive compulsion to acquire all items in games. Most of the overworld encounters felt meaningless and incredibly easy, while being entirely skip-able. I am not sure how to make those better, maybe less mobs and more difficult enemies.

While I enjoyed this game I do hope that FROMsoft leans away from open world games in the future.

[u]An absolute must‑play within the FromSoftware lineage.[/u]

Elden Ring takes the studio’s signature challenge‑driven design and expands it into a sweeping open world that feels endlessly explorable. While it departs from the tightly structured progression of Dark Souls 1–3 (a format I personally prefer) the shift still brings a unique strength. The Lands Between evoke the same sense of boundless possibility that made Skyrim so iconic, rewarding curiosity with challenging encounters, layered storytelling, and a level of discovery rarely matched in modern RPGs.

However, this sense of discovery didn't come without drawbacks. The sheer freedom of pathing makes FromSoftware’s already cryptic questlines even harder to track, often to the point of feeling overwhelming. NPC arcs can become fragmented or unintentionally skipped, and the open structure occasionally disrupts the narrative flow, creating moments where major story beats feel out of order or disconnected from the intended progression.

Regarding the graphics, it’s impossible not to highlight the sheer beauty of the world FromSoftware has crafted. Every vista, dungeon, and battlefield feels meticulously composed, often looking more like art then the video games of past. Whether standing still to admire the horizon or caught in the chaos of combat, nearly every moment feels screenshot-worthy. It’s a visually astonishing experience that elevates the sense of wonder throughout the journey.

Elden Ring is basically a beautiful open-world fantasy designed to repeatedly humble you until you somehow become the very demon that once bullied you. You start out as a weak little homeless corpse with  a stick getting folded by random soldiers, horses, dogs, gravity, and anything larger than a squirrel, while the game casually tells you to "go explore" like the entire map isn't trying to personally assassinate you. The bosses are incredible, mostly because they kill you so many times you develop a toxic relationship with them, but eventually the suffering pays off and suddenly a dual-wielding nightmare deleting enemies in three hits while dramatically walking through explosions. The world is gorgeous, the lore makes zero sense unless you watch a 6-hour video essay, and the online messages from other players range from helpful to clinically insane. We hated every second of getting destroyed...and immediately came back for more.

Elden Ring is simultaneously From Software's best and worst game. It is worth every penny spent and if you are looking for an answer to the question if you should buy it or not, look no further :

Go and play the game ! Maybe even consider using the Seamless Coop Mod to enjoy your time together with friends ! After all, this is From Software and the game won GOTY 2022 not without a good reason.

This being said, here is the critique i have to offer considering the highly debated topic of ranking the Souls born Games:
The Peaks are incredible, and the lows are mostly just minor annoyances, but it keeps an odd place in the Soulsborn lineup by being the only open world title so far. It is also the best or worst start you can have to the genre depending on what you enjoy in these games.
The major issues consist of the multiple reuse of the same bosses over and over again to a point where you are asking yourself if they even had enough time to finish the game or if they just filled holes before release.
Sometimes the reuse is explained and makes sense story-wise. Other times it is just as random as encountering a lion inside the supermarket next door. The same goes for some dungeons.
Controversially, the game gives the player the option to use spirit ashes as a way to make fights easier and more manageable, which also results in some fights feeling unbalanced if you don't rely on these mechanics to assist you (This argument only applies if you want the same journey as in the Dark Souls Series where u don't get Spirit Ashes to Assist you).
On the flip side of the coin, Elden Ring just delivers when it comes to visual designs as well as the boss design generally. The consistent and smooth combat allows the game to make enemies faster than previous titles and results in fights being exciting, punishing and ultimately more rewarding. It also shines when considering re-playability, since the game gets considerably more fun when you know which path to follow and which annoyances to avoid. This is paired with a huge number of weapons, spells and equipment which are balanced to make every build and play style viable. One can thus also heavily influence the difficulty of the game by changing the order in which to tackle the main tasks and spending an unlimited time leveling up in between bosses.

Therefore, i conclude the game to be a well-known friend of Schrödinger: The best and the worst Soulsborn game you might play, and you only know what it is once you look inside the Box.

My journey into Elden Ring began with confidence. I stepped into this majestic, mysterious world… and immediately locked eyes with a gigantic knight on a horse.

“Ah,” I thought. “You are mine.”

Reader, I was not his.

I charged in at level 1 like a hero in a prophecy. The prophecy was written by a liar. I died. Then I died again. And again. After ten attempts, I began to suspect that perhaps the game and I had… different expectations.

But here’s the trick: the world doesn’t shove you down a hallway. It whispers. Golden lights guide you forward, fires let you rest, and somewhere out there is your destiny… probably holding a weapon the size of a small building.

And scattered across this hauntingly beautiful world are messages from other players. Helpful guidance, you think.

“Treasure ahead.”

You follow it.

It’s a bloodstained cliff.

Another message:
“Try finger, but hole.”

No further explanation. Just vibes.

The gameplay loop is simple:
Fight. Roll. Die.
Fight better. Roll faster. Die with slightly more dignity.
Then suddenly… you don’t die. You win.

And when you level up enough, you return to that smug, oversized boss that bullied you earlier… and absolutely erase it from existence. Sweet, vengeful poetry.

Or, if you’re feeling less heroic, you summon help. And that help is almost always a nearly naked warrior with a pot on their head and two swords, who arrives like a feral tornado and deletes the boss while you’re still figuring out which button is “heal.”

Visually? Stunning. Every landscape looks like it was painted by a melancholic god.
Mechanically? Brutal. Your keyboard and mouse will develop trust issues.
Emotionally? YOU DIED.

After roughly 10,000 deaths, 4 playthroughs, multiple dragon-related incidents, and being humbled by creatures that might actually be gods…

I highly recommend it.

This is one of those games that stays with you long after you finish it. I completed Elden Ring on May 4, 2026, and honestly, the only word that fits is: peak.

I’ll be direct—I was wrong about this game at first. I went in expecting to dislike it, even hating on it early on. But somewhere between the struggle of the early hours and the intensity of the mid-to-late game, something changed. It didn’t just get better—it pulled me in completely. Looking back, I regret ever doubting it.

As my first Souls-like experience, this set a very high standard. I’d rate it an 8.5/10. Open-world games usually aren’t my preference, but that’s a personal bias—not a flaw of the game. In fact, the world design here proves why this genre works so well when done right.

What truly makes this game unforgettable is the feeling it gives you. The bosses are unique, challenging, and demanding. Every victory feels earned. When you finally overcome a difficult fight, it’s not just progress—it feels like a genuine achievement. Few games capture that sense of accomplishment this well.

Visually, the game is also stunning. If you can run it at max settings, the environments are nothing short of breathtaking, adding even more weight to every moment of exploration and combat.

I just finished it, and already I miss it. That says everything. This isn’t just a game you play—it’s one you experience and remember. And yes, beating Malenia? That’s a flex I’ll carry proudly.

If you’re reading this and wondering whether you should buy it: do it. Even if it looks difficult, even if you get stuck—it’s always possible to push through. And when you do, it’s worth it.

What a game to purchase on a whim, wow. I had never played Dark Souls or really any kind of Soulslike before, but the hype around Elden Ring was huge and I decided to just go for it, buying a copy for my PS5. I fell in love pretty quickly, the difficulty being just the right amount between genuinely challenging and thrilling and yet not exhaustively frustrating or insurmountable. The music is also 1000% my jam, so that was great too.

Eventually circumstances made me buy a Steam copy - it was just easier to play on my PC (or later, my Steam Deck) because I don't have ready access to a TV - so I started all over again and finally beat the game proper-like. Played through Shadow of the Erdtree, loved that too, and now I'm entering NG+ to get the other endings and play some of the Seamless Co-Op mod with a friend.

Elden Ring easily sits upon the Top 5 Favorite Games of All Time list for me, and probably held the #1 spot for a year and some change before Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 came and blew everything out of the water lol. I'm intrigued by Elden Ring: Nightreign, look forward to the next FromSoftware project, and have since expanded my Soulslike fare with Lies of P and a couple others on my to-do list like The First Berserker: Khazan.

A masterpiece. Playing on Xbox One at launch was rough due to performance issues (the skulls with runes couldn’t even spawn!).

Now, years later and playing on PC, I can engage with this game fully. And by doing so I have come to the conclusion that I prefer ‘Lies of P’. The parry mechanic in that game felt most rewarding, and the ability to retrieve your souls OUTside of a boss arena was appreciated. Should be a default mechanic. The combat, too, was less clunky. I sit here now having just fought a dragonkin boss in Elden Ring and realised that enemies large in scale make for poor melee combat, with me battling the lock-on system more than the enemy itself.

Loses half a star because, frankly, some of the questlines are ridiculous without a guide. I love an ‘information collective’ (Henry Jenkins) and the cryptic storyline is wonderful, but what do you mean I have to reload Fia three times over to experience the end of her questline. That’s just silly, and not obtuse in a fun way. Genuinely detracts from the overall experience and has meant that I’m rigorously following a step-by-step guide to experience everything in the game rather than just letting loose and exploring for my own sake.

Is that a me problem? Yes. But this is my review.

Elden Ring is widely considered one of the greatest video games of its generation, not because it is flawless, but because it successfully combines ambitious design, deep gameplay systems, and a uniquely rewarding sense of discovery into a single, cohesive experience. Developed by FromSoftware, the game builds upon the “Soulsborne” formula while expanding it into a massive open world, creating something that feels both familiar and groundbreaking at the same time.
One of the most important reasons Elden Ring is so good is its open-world design. Unlike many modern open-world games that rely heavily on markers and checklists, Elden Ring trusts the player to explore naturally. The world, known as the Lands Between, is vast and filled with hidden dungeons, bosses, and secrets. This creates a sense of genuine discovery—players are not just completing objectives, they are uncovering mysteries. Critics often describe the world as “rich” and “imaginative,” with an enormous scale that encourages curiosity and experimentation .
Another major strength is the gameplay itself, especially the combat system. Combat in Elden Ring is deliberate and challenging, requiring timing, patience, and skill. Every enemy encounter has weight, and every victory feels earned. The difficulty can be intimidating, but it is also what makes the experience so satisfying. As one review notes, mastering the combat provides a “profoundly satisfying” feeling that keeps players engaged despite repeated failures . This balance between difficulty and reward is a defining feature of the game.
Character customization and build variety also contribute to the game’s quality. Players can choose from a wide range of weapons, magic types, and playstyles, allowing for a highly personalized experience. Whether someone prefers melee combat, ranged attacks, or spellcasting, the game supports multiple approaches. This flexibility encourages replayability and experimentation, making each playthrough feel unique.
The artistic design and atmosphere are equally important. While Elden Ring may not be the most technically advanced game graphically, its art direction is widely praised. The environments—from haunting castles to golden fields—are visually striking and memorable. The game’s lore, partially written by George R. R. Martin, adds depth to the world, creating a sense of history and mystery that players piece together through exploration and subtle storytelling .
However, it is also important to acknowledge that Elden Ring is not perfect. Its difficulty can be a barrier for some players, and certain aspects of the open world can feel unclear or even frustrating. Some critics point out issues like uneven pacing, occasional technical problems, and moments where exploration feels aimless . These flaws prevent it from being universally accessible, but they also contribute to its identity as a challenging and uncompromising game.
In conclusion, Elden Ring is an exceptional game because it successfully blends exploration, challenge, and creativity into a single experience. It rewards curiosity, skill, and persistence in a way few games do. While it may not appeal to everyone, its ambition and execution have earned it a place among the best games ever made

Absolutely amazing game. Every time i get on, this game finds a new way to piss me off. I spent hours (HOURS!) trying to beat the "Fire Giant" just to finally beat him using a weapon called the "GodSkin Peeler" Ive had this game for about 4 days and i have already clocked 20 hours of gameplay. (That being said i am also employed) and i have never been so sure of a steam purchase in my life. 
10/10 would cry again. 


For people deciding to buy the game: I play a lot of games and i have never played a game that has so much depth and story. Multiple ending depending on what side quests you do, different sorceries and incantations that you cant get unless you did the questline (Some questlines go away if you do another one kind of like picking sides in a war) New Game + is the only way to get another questlines items and another ending and you can just keep beating it as it gets harder and harder. When you are starting for your first time you will have absolutely no idea what to do (DO NOT HIT OR KILL THE GUY IN THE WHITE MASK WHEN YOU GET OUT OF THE TUTORIAL, He will hunt you down, you WILL always be looking over your shoulder. I recommend looking at "Get a very strong build in 30 minutes" by BastosFC2 (Not my channel was just a great guide in the beginning) It will guide you through how to get a samurai build that does pretty decent most of the game. 

Anyways, I had an amazing time venturing through the Elden Ring world finding new items i had to google what to do with, And all the lore/stories that is built into this very intense game. If you have a lot of time on your hands (and a LOT of patience) then you should absolutely without a doubt get Elden Ring and start that 2 week phase with your friends (speaking of friends there is a Seamless Co-op mod that allows you to play with your friends)

No new achievements have been added since the base game yet I still find myself coming back to this. A testament to how good this game is. The game is probably the best in the AAA industry right now and not even close. It's replayable, the lore is amazing, it's story is extremely compelling, and most importantly, IT'S ACTUALLY FUN! A rare sight to see for modern AAA games.

As for SOTE, it's an incredible DLC that shines a lot of light on certain gaps in the lore from the base game. The gameplay is fantastic, the bosses are stupidly difficult even in the current version. I am currently playing an armorless run and I only have two bosses from the expansion left and they are incredibly difficult. Even with armor they would probably still destroy me just because of how difficult they are to learn.

This is another one of the few games I can recommend that will take a long time to complete just because it's so engaging and makes every hour feel worth it.

Where do I begin? By the time I was done with it, the game left me completely disappointed. Most of the negative reviews have captured all the significant problems with this game so I'll keep it (relatively) short.

My favorite games in the Fromsoft catalogue are Dark Souls and Sekiro (also among my favorite games of all time). These games feel deliberately designed; Dark Souls made you feel like exploring a dangerous land, and Sekiro's combat system is truly one of a kind. But Elden Ring? It's a video game ass video game. 

Everything in this game exists for the player's benefit. Side dungeons almost always end in boss fights with a treasure chest to reward the player. Stakes of Marika make it convenient to fight bosses. Sites of Grace abound so that you never feel the tension of exploration (also free fast travel). Every single death in this game will only be due to bosses, or sometimes gravity. Don't wanna fight overworld enemies? Get on Torrent and ride away.

The open world is simply a collection of all the dungeons they made spread horizontally. Is the open world truly open then? Nope, there's a strictly linear progression with some side stuff along the way, with arbitrary progression gates like Morgott's Seal and Great rune requirements. Balance is literally non-existent. 

Is the combat good then? It's basically Dark Souls combat again. There's a gazillion weapons, but they all mostly play the same, you know how it is. Boss fights are now true shonen anime style affairs, wait for your turn and dodge while the boss does his wombo combo and then poke. Repeat all over again for a 100 bosses, and the nail in the coffin is that a significant portion of them are repeats. Not even remembrance bosses are spared.

Don't even get me started on the DLC.

I was genuinely excited to play this game, and sadly, this made me question the future of Soulslikes from Fromsoft. I guess we'll have to wait and see.

I purchased Elden Ring and got around to playing it recently, only to find the game freezing for 2 to 3 seconds every few minutes, making it completely unplayable. This is a well-documented issue with countless forum threads and YouTube videos confirming it. My machine had no trouble running Spider-Man 2, Black Myth: Wukong, or Lies of P. The problem is clearly with the game itself.

What makes this especially frustrating is that this is not a new or obscure bug. Players have been reporting this freezing issue for a long time, and FromSoftware has done little to nothing to address it. When a problem is this widespread and this well known, leaving it unresolved is not an oversight, it is a choice. Selling a product you know is broken for a significant portion of your playerbase and doing nothing about it is simply not acceptable. At some point, the developer and the platform that profits from selling it share responsibility for that.

Of my 2 hours and 57 minutes of playtime, the vast majority was spent attempting to debug the freezing issue, not actually playing the game. I requested a refund, not because I didn't want to play it, but because I couldn't. Steam denied it, citing their 2-hour/14-day policy without any acknowledgment of the circumstances.

The support experience was worse than the technical issue. Three different agents gave me copy-paste responses. One assured me refunds are considered "case by case" but offered zero explanation of what factors were actually weighed. When I asked them to consider that I only launched the game for the first time days ago, or that nearly 3 hours on a 100+ hour game hardly constitutes taking advantage of their policy, I got silence. Then they closed the ticket.

As a software engineer, I'd never tell my customers to debug my product on their own time. Steam apparently has no such qualms. The platform I trusted to protect my purchases hid behind a rigid policy to retain a modest amount of money from a customer with a completely legitimate complaint about a broken product.

If you're thinking about buying a game and playing it at your own pace, the way gaming used to work, think twice. Steam's refund policy doesn't protect that. Buy it, launch it immediately, and decide within 2 hours or you're on your own.

I truly love this game.

My first souls-like as it isn't exactly my type of routines, and thus is this review's perspective:
- It's beautiful. It's a relaxing exploration of a dying world with spam-able mobs.
- It's annoying. It gets cheeky with mobs that don't play the same rules as others or yours.
- It's repetitive. 50 easier mobs only require the same moves repeatedly (usually stun-locked and can't survive a full combo). Cheekier mobs can only be fought with the same exploitive moves 50 times. They respawn.
- The mob diversity and how that tells the story is quite amazing.
- The combat control is not very responsive and balance is not intuitive. It's a long list.
- Fighting dragons aren't the problem. They're more like learning music. The real challenges are fighting your own character's animation sets (about 3 out of 5 animations are self-destructive), animation queues and delays that triggers after your current (very slow) one, and the camera looking too far up or too far down, blinding you. I lost that fight often.
- I've rage-quit only twice. Once was the first 1.5 hours when a horny guy at a large castle ♥♥♥♥-blocking me, who eventually taught me to explore elsewhere rather than follow "directions" (basically, "Don't trust the game"), which I ended up enjoying quite a bit. Then another an hour ago when I got too bored with the routines and started rushing bosses and seeing if I can use any other moves beyond 2 for 10 minutes each, which was a stern "no, don't trust the game". Considering the reputation of souls-like, I thought that wasn't so bad. Though, I was hoping that'd be due to some difficulty barrier, not how counter-intuitive it felt, especially after so many of these games the company has been making and how well received they have been.

Souls-like is still not my type of game. But I know many people enjoy these type of routines, and I do enjoy watching them playing or analyzing.

A Late but Honest Review of Elden Ring

This is a very late review, so you'll easily find better and more detailed ones out there. Still, here are my thoughts after finishing the base game and working through the DLC.Elden Ring was my first proper FromSoftware game, though I had already played several souls-likes (Hollow Knight, Blasphemous, and others).

The Good:
The base game is excellent. There's a huge amount to do, the world looks stunning, and the bosses are the highlight — they're epic, memorable, and the main reason to keep playing.

The world design is outstanding. It's beautifully interconnected, and if you can see a location in the distance, you can usually find a way to get there. It gives a real sense of freedom and exploration, similar to Breath of the Wild.

Combat is intense and punishing, which is both its greatest strength and biggest point of frustration. Enemies are aggressive, and groups can quickly overwhelm you if you're not careful. There are also quite a few "cheap" enemy placements, traps, and surprise attacks that feel unfair at times. That said, playing completely blind, I found it to be a hard but mostly fair challenge overall.One standout feature is the enemy variety: almost every regular enemy has around 15 different attack animations, and bosses often have double that. It makes fights feel dynamic and replayable.

The Not-So-Good:
The story is my biggest complaint. It's extremely vague and unclear for the first 40+ hours. Even after that, huge parts remain unexplained, forcing you to watch video essays or read wikis to understand what's actually going on. Many systems, item descriptions, and lore details are so obscure that it's often hard to know what you're supposed to do or why something matters. If I could change one thing about the game, it would be making the story and key information clearer. 

Crafting exists but can largely be ignored if you don't enjoy it. However, I strongly recommend paying attention to weapon upgrades using smithing stones. I neglected them for too long and definitely suffered for it. 

Music and sound design are solid and atmospheric, but not particularly memorable or thematic. 

Performance is decent (see pc specs) but not perfect. Frame rate dips into the low 40s (and sometimes lower) can happen, especially during intense boss fights or in the DLC areas. It’s noticeable and occasionally frustrating.

DLC:
The Shadow of the Erdtree DLC is very much worth the price. It's nearly as large as the base game and feels like a full expansion. I’d recommend saving it until you’ve nearly finished (or fully completed) the main game. Very similar in quality to the Witcher's blood and wine expansion.

Final Thoughts:
Is Elden Ring the greatest game of all time? Not for me. Would I tell anyone to avoid it? Absolutely not. It has an incredible amount of quality and allure. The exploration, bosses, and sense of discovery are top-tier.That said, story and clear motivation are what keep me most engaged in games, and in that regard, The Witcher 3 is still my gold standard. Elden Ring doesn’t come close there.If I had to give it a score, I’d say a strong 9/10. Pro tip: Install a simple boss tracker app on your phone (the game doesn’t track progress for you), and don’t be afraid to look up guides when you get stuck. It’ll save you a lot of frustration without spoiling the magic.

At first, you will be overwhelmed by the world, its lore, and its difficulty. You will lose your bearings and spend your time chasing better gear. After grinding endlessly, you will eventually come to your senses, only to be stunned once again by the beautiful landscapes around you. As you keep pushing forward, you will begin to notice the unique philosophies and habits of the characters, their wishes, their hopes.

As you advance and come to understand the history, you may find yourself wanting to become a good ruler. You may immerse yourself in one path, then another, concerning every ending, until you begin to wonder:

"Was there ever any meaning?"

"In this world ruined by ambition, by division, by internal strife, was there ever meaning at all?" 

... You may sink even deeper into the world and continue to wonder. And if you do, then you are ready to define chaos. 

In a world that cannot be beautiful without division, separation, pain, and suffering, for the sake of dignifying sacrifices. 

Purify it with fire, the pure chaos of flame.

The dreams of the ladies who stole your heart, the motives behind those dreams, the seeds of their pain, their courage to reach for the abundance, their hope, their sacrifice.

All of it becomes possibility itself beneath pure chaos.

The first seed planted in the world is like desire itself, fingers stretching out in swarms, growing, breeding, multiplying.

The hearts of godhood and every beings are no different.

Burn the roots.

You can do it.

You, of all people, can.

As the Outer God of frenzied flame,

As the golden blaze,

As the One before the beginning

Burn it all away.

Burn it all.

The best RPG and Action game I have ever played so far. At the time I am writing this, I have finished two playthroughs and I am getting ready for a third. This is also the first Souls-like I have finished (excluding Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice)

Starting off with a few cons:
* The combat system is not beginner friendly, but given you have the time and patience, it is definitely worth the time learning it.
* There are also some bosses with annoying gimmicks such as [spoiler]the Godskin Duo and sometimes the Elden Beast[/spoiler], but the game provides you with tools to help you in areas that you struggle.
* The mechanics are not perfect by any means. Some bosses take up a majority of your screen or lack a good enemy lock-on feature, leaving you with little visibility. 
* There are some armor sets and weapons that you may have to grind unreasonable amounts of time for, as those have very low drop rates. Fortunately, you are not forced to grind for specific items to make progress, but it is frustrating spending a lot of time doing repetitive grinding for an item you want.

Now, for the pros of this game:
* Very fun progression. It is really fun to see the story progress, as well as seeing what other areas and equipment you can find as you progress.
* Great character aesthetics. The character creator is incredibly fun to mess with and you can make some really nice or even funny looking characters. There are also dozens of armor sets and equipment that you can mix and match to express yourself fully.
* Very fun build system. There are thousands of possibilities for different builds you can make and styles you can use. The game provides you with weapons that you can change the affinity of as well as abilities (some special weapons are not customizable, though). You may also use different types of spells (offensive and defensive) along those weapons.
* Very fun combat system. Not only do you have access to different equipment and stats, but you can also fight enemies in ways that you find most fun. Bosses are really engaging and challenging, but still allow for players to express themselves with different builds.
* Beautiful visuals. The art direction for the world, enemies, and player equipment are amazing. The graphics fidelity is great as well even at lower settings.

Overall, this is my number 1 favorite game so far (tied with Terraria for very similar reasons), and I am looking forward to trying out other FromSoft titles.

Man... I really wanted to like this game, I love other souls-likes and was so hyped to play this, but it just felt so disrespectfull of player's time. The open world was overambitious and it lead to most of the game feeling empty and many many enemies even bosses being recycled.
Despite what people say about fast-paced combat, a huge portion of the experience is spent riding around on your horse wondering where to go next.
I put 132 hours into this game, and honestly, it didn’t feel worthwhile. I kept expecting it to get better or deliver some kind of payoff that justified the time investment, but it never really did.
The boss design felt excessive towards the end of the game, where it's just one brutal bossfight after another all of which felt like a chore. Usually in games like this there's a satisfaction from beating a tough boss, and you look back fondly on the fight. But the last few bosses I'd finish and just feel that my time had been wasted.
By the end, this felt like something I had to finish rather than something I wanted to finish. Instead of improving on the Souls formula, it felt like it watered it down and soured it.

Planning on coming back and updating this review at some point because I know its a bit unfair of me to rate it negatively after having only scratching the surface of the game (from what I've heard/seen) and only having played it for 4 hours BUT: within those 4 hours I barely got to play the actual game. I don't have much experience with souls-likes but I have played my fair share of RPGs. In fact, one of the things I hate the most about most modern RPGs is the amount of hints and 'recommended paths' they do. I don't need a hint to pop up when I can't solve a puzzle within the first 30 seconds of encountering it and I don't need NPCs to use no-so-subtle dialogue to tell me which path may be harder or which path will have more dire consequences. At this point if you're a fan of souls-like games you might be thinking "Oh Elden ring would be perfect for you! There's no hand holding at all." Except no hand holding doesn't have to mean no tutorial/unrepeatable tutorials and bad interface design, but it does in this game. I don't know if that's because this game may not have been originally intended for PC and therefore may have been poorly adapted for mouse and keyboard players but in my experience so far, it's really sucked. Boxes that should be clickable aren't, you can't use the escape key to exit out of some menus, you can't pause the game even though its just you playing, among other pains. Now I know some players bask in the brutality of it but the reality is that its a primarily single player game, meaning if players deviate from the intended way to play, who cares? They're enjoying the game the way they want to. I personally have never 'pause-buffered' or used menu shortcuts to give me an advantage against difficult enemies in any game, nor do I intend to, but not being able to pause a single player game when some sort of IRL circumstance calls is quite frustrating. Additionally, the tutorial sucks and returning to the game is incredibly difficult. I have tried returning to this game twice now since I first played it, each time giving it roughly an hour to an hour and a half. Both times I have spent nearly 30 minutes trying to understand the combat mechanics and the equipment menu. It's not that either is incredibly difficult to use, it's that they are poorly explained and difficult to learn. I've played several other games that have the formula of 'dodge roll' 'heavy attack' 'light attack' 'block/parry' but in all of them all I have to do to learn what buttons do what is hit the pause menu and look at controls. In Elden Ring, even when I do that it simply lists 'dodge roll' 'strafe' 'backstep' as one button. And on top of that  that same button is used to turn light attacks into heavy attacks. Sure I can adapt, figure out what conditions need to be met for my character to perform a 'strafe' instead of a 'dodge roll' or a 'backstep', but I don't want to relearn every single time I return. And if you're thinking "Huh, what kinda guy only plays a game for an hour or two then doesn't come back to it for a month??" I'd agree with you because normally I do enjoy a game for a full playthrough, however many hours that may take, but with Elden ring, every time I've given it a try, I've experienced more frustration than I have had fun. So when I come back to my computer for the next session a day later, I decide not to play it. And games are meant to be fun. So maybe your personal takeaway can be that you need to give it more time to transition from frustration to fun than I did.

This game holds a very special place in my heart as the first FromSoftware game that I played, especially since I first played it after searching for the next game to play like BOTW and TOTK. I've bought the game twice now (first on console where I have hundreds of hours) and now on Steam, and I've fallen back in love with it after having taken a break for a little while, and I have to say, there really is nothing quite like it. It is the only 10/10 game I have ever played in my opinion, and that 10/10 score is well deserved, the game does everything perfectly, between the amazing open world that has been masterfully crafted, and the infinite replayability with its innumerable builds and styles of play. On top of that, the story of this game runs extremely deep, and is incredibly interesting (I highly recommend checking out some Youtube videos on the topic). But what I think Elden Ring does the best is the design of the world itself, and the life that is scattered throughout it. It does what BOTW and TOTK couldn't quite accomplish, in that it spreads enemies and NPCs out enough so that they aren't crowding each other, while leaving nowhere so devoid of life that it is boring for the player, and makes sure that there is always something for the player to do that isn't tedious or just straight up annoying. I truly cannot recommend it enough, 10/10 and my favorite game of all time.

What a great game that I wish I had picked up sooner!

What kept me away from FromSoft game's was the complete hard-stop I experienced back with DS1 (PTDE) at Blight Town.  Manditory difficult jump(s), poison, with high fall probability in a scaffolding-type ladder/maze area... died so many times until I simply got frustrated and uninstalled the game... although I had thoroughly enjoyed it up to that point.

Things I love about this game:
1) large beautiful open world just begging to be explored... having trouble with a boss? go explore, level up, and come back later
2) exploration is often well rewarded
3) tonnes of sorceries, incantations, armor, weapons, Ashes of War, and summons to mix'n' match for a variety of play-styles and builds
4) crafting can turn pickups into useful items... like bombs, cures, xp boost, item find boost, etc.
5) map marker system super helpful
6) There are "natural" spots in the game near site's of grace that make "time efficient" XP farming "easy" when you were either (a) looking to reach item stat requirements, (b) wanted to force a level up to spend all your runes before attempting a difficult fight, or (c) needed more runes to purchase items.  The first spot I used was inside a castle just outside the final boss room... there was a giant there just down the steps that I could kill easily, rest, and repeat whenever I wanted/needed more runes.

What i wish the game had:
1) a side quest tracker / journal - the site's of grace point you along the main story path, but you have to manually make note of "side quest details" you find via conversations with NPC's... if they mention a place/person make sure you come back to talk to them later after you've visited who/where they the mentioned to move the side quest along.  Sometimes it's hard to know if the conversation was just delivering background/lore or giving you a side quest.

Things I'm not a fan of:
1) side quests can auto-advance / skip milestones if you pushed ahead too far either via exploration or progressing the main quest.  very easy to do if you didn't realize you had a side quest with the absence of a quest tracker / journal
2) I generally don't do well with "jumping puzzles" and die... so more a me issue than the game.  There are a number of spots/areas where parkour (and selective falling) is important... especially if you want to explore/traverse all areas... so far I've managed to accomplish most without too many deaths.

Things I hate about the game:
1) Nothing comes to mind.

At the time of writing this review I have yet to complete the game.  I'm nearing 130 hours on my second character... I abondoned the first character (80-90 hrs) after figuring out they had "botched side quests".  I've yet to decide if I'm going to try the "backup/restore my save file" method for reaching all the endings or if I'll just replay the game 3 times... I have a feeling I'd replay this game anyway, but getting the achievements out of the way in this playthourgh (via save backup/restore) could take the pressure off and/or eliminate the possibility of not unlocking or choosing the desired next ending achievement by mistake.

this playthrough makes mee wish there was a middle option for reviewing.

this time i wanted to explore everything and do everything, i just gave up half way through and went to beat the main bosses and finish the story. you can make fun of me for saying there is too much ♥♥♥♥ to do in this game. but there really is.

my latest playthrough ends at like 127 hours and i stopped bothering to explore the second half of the game and it's areas. i once again get to radhan at the dlc's final boss, but give up on it because i don't think it is worth trying to finish. if it is your first time, it is really cool, it's amazing and there is so much to do you can just do it forever. once i get the stuff i like, i don't want to waste time to go places when i won't get anything good.

so graphically, it is a great looking game, it all looks dark and gritty, but clean and nice at the same time, areas are unique and diverse and all are interesting to explore and see the unique architecture of it all. weapons all looks nice, and there are some that are phenomenal. 

the music is always good, it is one of fromsoft's best details in all their games. from the simple minimalist music of the areas which really help give you subtle ideas of how it feels, and can get your into the mood of how wrong a place actually would feel if you were there. it is incredibly immersive and it doesn't have to blast full soundtracks at you to get the idea. then you get slightly more engaging combat music which lets you know you are currently in danger, and then the fantastic boss music. it is just always so good to listen to and some bosses are elevated because of how good their track is.

gameplay wise, i have some problems here, coming off of playing all the souls games recently. this one feels good and has a good speed to it. the weapons are honestly all fun, i used more weapons this time than i ever did and even basic variants, with customizing the weapons skills can make it really fun. wether you use new things, unique weapons, legendary, or the basic ones fitted to your playstyle and preference.

my problem is, in the base game it really isn't THAT bad, there are some annoying things but it can kind of be overlooked. mostly poise being a complete joke and being absolutely meaningless unless you wear heavy armor, as well as getting lots of high poise enemies late game which makes you just want to use heavy weapons yourself because they tank through anything that isn't a heavy attack from anything small.

the dlc messed me up, and i shouldn't have entered it before i beat the main game because i burned out on exploration and using items/weapons as i played through it. it also bring a new scaling system which makes a lot of things obscenely too strong until you explore around enough and find the scaling items to boost your level, and even then sometimes it doesn't even feel like it is enough to even the playing field.

while i would personally say that like 50-60% of the base game is amazing and the other 40% is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ just to make the game feel more difficult than it already is, or the lock on system absolutely ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ itself whenever an enemy has a long neck. like ancient dragons, they have two lock on points and one you can't hit because it is too high while the head is too far in front of the body so the camera likes to face the opposite way meaning you can't see anything the enemy is doing. the dlc i would say is more ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ than fun because of scaling, and a lot of enemy designs. bosses are cool and cinematic, but even tough enemies later into the dlc are awful designed. bosses that move super fast, read all your inputs and have such fast attacks that you can't avoid them without constant roll catching, or just decimate any shield you bring because of how much they attack. fire knights are abysmal because of their infinite attacking they can do. not only are they fast but they can dodge and summon homing magic that has a delay to it. so they catch you in a loop of dodge until they decide to get close, or risk getting it and taking a lot of damage just to try to get them to stop.

the last thing i think to talk about would be the exploration. there is so much of it and it is nice, the world ids packed with groups that roam around or people on patrol or sitting around. it really does feel like it is lived in. but there is so much that you spend so much time running around looking for things and exploring and it honestly feels like a good portion of what you do is just filler for the actual content of the game. there were too many times where you would explore somewhere thinking there could be something cool only to find either nothing. or needlessly tough enemies that aren't worth fighting, that end up sitting around a single crafting item. which if you use that system, it might be useful. but it got to a point where i picked up crafting materials and never opened my menu again. so it was essentially meaningless junk. just to make you feel like you were being rewarded.

like. this game is great, i like it. but the amount of time it takes to full clear and explore, and the enemy designed can really hurt the game and it's enjoyment and really made me just not care about fighting a lot of bosses or going through a lot of areas. since it just kind of got to a point where i felt like i was going to do these things and waste my time most of  the time.

at first i always thought it was dumb to look up a guide to find things in souls games, but replaying elden ring this time makes me appreciate that you can just skip all the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ and filler and get right to the good stuff that will help you enjoy the game better.

it's worth it to play if you want. and while i am not the sweatiest thousand hour gamer on this game, i still find these games can be hard, challenging, or fun because of this. but i feel like this one would be the hardest of all of them, so keep that in mind if you weren't sure if you would want to play this or one of the souls series instead.

This massive RPG delivers on the promise of a vast, breathing, living world to explore. Everything feels alive and you do, indeed, feel like an outsider while playing. This "me against the world" theme is common in FromSoft games, and the formula works. The only thing I take issue with are the moments of the game which expect you to (for example) find a hidden wall in a random room to access a whole other region of the map. I understand that this is appealing for some, but I simply do not have the time to explore every nook and cranny of every building in this game as it is gargantuan. Nevertheless, the boss-fights are fun and can be as challenging as you like; NG+ offers a cool way to replay the game too, and completely restarting on a fresh save offers a chance to find hidden content because there is lots. As someone who has only previously played Sekiro, this was a 4 out of 5 star game for me. However, I do understand that I am not the target audience for this game. As such, I assume that if you enjoy the FromSoft formula, then you will love this.

One Ring made by the evil lord Sauron Hidetaka Miyazaki, One Ring to rule them all, One Ring to find them, One Ring to bring them all and in the darkness bind them, In the Land of Japanese software development warehouse building where Shadows lie.

Elden Ring is not worth your time due to the profound curse placed upon the game's core design by the evil man who made it. The game aims at being infinitely replayable, but each of its assets is designed in a narrow fashion - so much so that the world does not feel as open as it is rather episodic. Each and every instance in the game is instrumented to be seen and encountered in a specific way which makes the experience of world exploration predictable and less exciting after playing for the first time. The dificulty ranges of the various explorable regions of the game world are awfully imballanced, so much so that it's less enjoyable to avoid choosing a linear path through the game on later playthroughs. The game world's incredibly size makes this issue even more frustrating - in order to experience something new on a subsequent playthrough, the player has to cross massive distances which are increasingly less and less aproachable the further he is from the starting point, ultimately discouraging exploration and narrowing the game experience to a single, most optimalized path of progression. There are only so many ways to reexperience the game's world without being severely discouraged and punished. And so ultimately, the game world's size and the quantity of its assets is severely nullified as the various paths to approach them are tragically so very limited.

The game's world becomes eventually too predictable and the premise of experiencing the game in a fresh way nonexistent, which is not fun. Let it be pronounced and heard, Elden Ring could be legendary, but it is not, and won't be, unless FromSoftware developpers cease to practice the habit of forgetting a game once it's been created, despite the quantity of bothersome features still present and still widely acknowledged. One may recall the pathos of never properly finishing Lost Izalith in their previous game Dark Souls, an area of the game so very unique and all the more so disappointing for being critically underdevelopped. I feel very simillar about Elden Ring.

As a former world of warcraft player, I see fit to pronounce a variety of ideas which could severely improve the experience of Elden Ring if they were ever developped, as they are present in that particular game, and although a very different game at that, still possesing a massive world and the spirit of an rpg game. 

Those ideas would be:
There should be a variety of start points in Elden Ring, or at the very least, it should be possible to cross into other regions of the game from Limgrave without being stupefied and severely punished as much as is the case now. There should be more low level regions surrounding Limgrave so as to increase replayability and the possibilities of exploration on future playthroughs. And yes, despite the existence of various teleportation chests in Limgrave, the fact that the places you are sent to are completely above Limgrave's difficulty makes them less relevant as a travel feature. There is not enough enemy variety at each of the game's various places - an occasional "rare enemy spawn" or the promise of a "rare item drop" would be a solution to this issue. And lastly, there should be more paths connecting the various regions and places of the Elden Ring world and more accessible ways to connect the game's many features.

In conclusion, Elden Ring nowhere near as fun as enjoyable after the first couple of playthroughs, it becomes stale and predictable rather too fast, and that is a completely nonsensical feature for a game of such high ambition and such quantity of the game's features.

Before anything, GET A CONTROLLER. I thought that Mouse and Keyboard will always be the way to go and that controller is for lazy people, GET THE CONTROLLER. I was only able to put in like 2 hours on MnK before I got the to point that I simply could not play anymore, I felt too clunky and combat was agony. GET. THE. CONTROLLER. 

Now on to the actual review.

This game is unlike anything I have ever played before. It literally just launches you into the most hostile, uninviting, and dangerous setting and that's it. All you have is the "Armor" you are wearing and the will to become the Elden Lord. This game will NOT hold your hand, and it might seem weird and awkward at first but it is the best thing they could have done. The exploring alone is unparalleled, the map is deceptively ENORMOUS, and trust me, if its your first run, you will not BELIEVE the magnitude of this game. 

The boss fights.
Cinematic. One of the biggest reasons I die so much is because I get so distracted by the beautiful animations of the boss fights, its incredibly hard not to with the flashes of light and movement sequencing some of these bosses have. Are the fights hard? ABSOLUTELY! Are they incredibly frustrating? YOU BET! Does being stun locked and hit by 6 hits in a row when you accidentally ran out of stamina and held your shield and get killed when the boss was literally one shot and now you need to get a new controller cuz yours somehow ended up smashed to pieces on the floor make you want to rip out your hair? Yeah... But when you finally defeat it? BLISS

Be patient, put in the hours. oh my god its so worth it. Since I got my controller 2 days ago i've but almost 30 hours into the game, and I selfishly wish I wasn't an actual adult with a wife so I could sink in 20 Hours a day into this masterpiece and not worry about anything else because I literally DREAM of this game when I am not playing it. 
Huge reccomend.

I initially bought this in 2024, playing it for about 50 hours before stopping and resuming back in March-April of 2026. It didn't click for me until i respec'd my build into a battle-mage, and that's when i fell in love with the game, leading me to sink 80 more hours (and more since i plan to start NG+) and grab the DLC.

I also recommend playing it completely blind, as to not ruin the fun of surprises and being side-tracked every five minutes. If you can't help it, a walkthrough video or two is fine since their quest system is verbose and tedious, barely offering you guidance to get some specific items. It baffles me on how some people are meant to discover certain quest paths.

Despite the steep learning curve for first time souls-players, this game is very much worth it. If you are skeptical, buy it on sale. The DLC is also worth the steep price tag. As of this review, it has never gone on sale, so don't bother waiting for one and only question yourself if you'd like more Elden Ring before buying the DLC.

this might be one of the worst games i've ever played; i genuinely don't see where the glaze comes from. i always try to look for positives in a game first, but it's hard to find ANYTHING to like about elden ring. no, i did not finish the game, but with a game like elden ring you don't need to finish it to gauge its quality. after all the game is extremely repetitive and lackluster (an excuse of an open world game), which is one of my main gripes.
i don't inherently have an issue with parries in combat systems, but the way that elden ring does it is not satisfying at all and feels extremely forced and, not only skill-less, but also soulless. sure, you can dodge attacks, or make a really cool build, but in the end the gameplay always bogs down to, "parry, swing, swing, parry, swing, occasional unparriable attack, swing, swing, big stun damage choreographed attack..." repeat until you either die or win. there isn't really much merit in making varied builds if it doesn't change the gameplay at all. that's like, if when equipping a sword as demoman in tf2, instead of swinging the sword, you throw it in an arc and get headshots for more damage. that's effectively what elden ring is with its build "variety". or, of course, you could just make a super OP build... but then where's the gameplay? what SKILL are you guys seeing in this? learning enemy patterns doesn't count, because you'll be doing that in ANY game. ergo, there is nothing that makes elden ring's combat unique save for its own poor game design.
the world is pretty good looking, but it felt extremely empty and unrewarding to explore. you could either find some niche enemy to fight, or a guy that sells you incrementally better weapons, or both, or neither. maybe it gets better as you explore the map. but the open world shouldn't force me to progress its story for it to... open the world (side note, i don't mean the game can't gradually open up its world or be something similar to BOTW, but one, i was already WAY past the tutorial, and two, it isn't acceptable when there isn't anything to explore in the first place.)
i can't speak on story much, but from what i have seen, it feels extremely misguided, fragmented and meandering. what is the message of elden ring's story? i get that not every story needs to have some super impactful message, but, if elden ring isn't one of those games, what is there to merit about its story? the fact it has cool character designs? you can go to some random place and find a tiny piece of lore? this isn't ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ undertale, it doesn't work.

seriously i do not recommend this game to anyone who has any self respect and actually wants the games they play to be of quality and substance. this might work as parryslop for the majority, but it really isn't all that.

Elden Ring is the greatest game ever made. Period. Almost everyone here in the reviews are saying the same thing, it's basically fact. It's tied with Portal for being my favorite. It is FromSoft's magnum opus. I struggled to get into this game for over three years. I originally got the game near launch, and it was my introduction to the Soulsbourne series. I knew they were hard games, but I didn't expect it to be so hard to GET into. The performance wasn't great on my old system which also hindered my progression. I put it down. I started playing other games that didn't challenge me. They were easy in comparison, easy to play, easy to enjoy, just...easy. I felt a little empty, defeated, and I felt bored. I bought Sekiro knowing it's different to the other games in the series thinking maybe this one's for me. It turned out to be the most difficult of the bunch and I didn't get too far lol. But a few months ago I re-downloaded Elden Ring (I had 3 hours on record), and gave it another shot. I had a beefier PC and I just felt the FOMO, because I knew it got amazing reviews. I stepped into the Lands Between and I was hooked. 

I failed for hours, I shut down the voice in my head saying "told you so", I shut down the rage, and I took a deep breath and went again and again. I beat the first boss and I felt an adrenaline run, this high that felt more powerful, more invigorating than my addiction. I beat the next boss, and the next and the next. I was hooked. I hooked on the process of beating them. I got to the end of the game in tears not only for the masterpiece this game is in story, gameplay, world building etc. but also my progression as a human being. I'd grown so much. I was in therapy for my health during this whole time and in every session I was relating my health to Elden Ring. I was obsessed, obsessed with the real life results. I felt purpose in areas I lacked purpose before. I tackled my issues like I tackled bosses. I learnt their weaknesses from failing again and again, I learnt openings, I learnt control, and patience. I wanted to beat this game (addiction) so hard I could beat anything else thrown my way. I've been clean for six months now and I am so filled with joy and hope. 

I know I spoke about mainly me in this review, but we already know about how amazing this game is, and I think it is important to share our experiences with it. I can definitively say this game is art, for the reasons listed above. This game is perfect and I will forever cherish my time with it, wishing only to experience it all again for the first time. 

Good luck, have fun. Don't you dare go hollow.
